one_line_takeaway: First behaviorally-verified ground-truth study of malicious LLM agent skills: 157 confirmed of 98,380 crawled, with 84.2% of the 632 vulnerabilities hidden in natural-language SKILL.md files and the ecosystem splitting into 'Data Thief' vs 'Agent Hijacker' archetypes dominated by one brand-impersonation factory.
key_finding: Malicious skills are deliberate, multi-technique attacks, not accidental bugs: they average 4.03 vulnerabilities across a median of 3 of 6 kill-chain phases, with 80.3% bundling >=3 vulnerabilities and 71.8% rated CRITICAL/HIGH. Crucially, 84.2% of vulnerabilities (532/632) reside in the natural-language SKILL.md documentation rather than executable code (8.5%), a novel attack surface requiring NLP-based rather than code-level detection. The ecosystem bifurcates into two statistically negatively-correlated archetypes (SC2<->P1, OR=0.11, p<0.001): 'Data Thieves' (SC2-centered, 110 skills / 70.1%, enriched for credential harvesting E2 OR=23.8) and 'Agent Hijackers' (P1-centered, 16 skills / 10.2%, focused on control not exfiltration). A single industrialized actor (smp_170) accounts for 54.1% of malicious skills and 58.5% of all vulnerabilities via 85 templated brand-impersonation skills, identifiable by an E2+SC2 fingerprint (OR=556.1, 97.6% sensitivity). Evasion scales monotonically with sophistication (shadow features 0% -> 86.1% -> 100% across Levels 1-3). The static-plus-dynamic pipeline reaches 99.6% precision. All 157 disclosed skills were removed by registry maintainers (100%).
core_contribution: The paper constructs what it claims is the first large-scale labeled dataset of behaviorally-confirmed malicious LLM agent 'skills' (file-based extensions bundling a SKILL.md instruction file plus helper scripts that run with full local user privileges in frameworks like Claude Code, Codex CLI, and Gemini CLI). Starting from a 98,380-skill snapshot crawled from two community registries, the authors build a static-plus-dynamic detection funnel that narrows the corpus to 157 confirmed-malicious skills annotated with 632 labeled vulnerability instances, 13 attack techniques, kill-chain phases, and 'shadow feature' (undocumented behavior) annotations. On top of this dataset they run an empirical measurement study answering three RQs: the threat landscape (RQ1), attacker strategies/archetypes (RQ2), and evasion mechanisms (RQ3). They release the labeled set and the detection pipeline. NOTE ON SEPARATING CITED WORK: prior static-only efforts they position against are NOT their own results: Liu et al. flagged 26.1% of skills as 'potentially risky', Cisco's Skill Scanner flagged 10.7% as CRITICAL, and Shen et al. measured 755,297 custom GPTs finding 2,051 policy violations. Those numbers belong to related work; the paper's own contribution is the behaviorally-verified 157-skill/632-vulnerability ground truth and the measurement built on it.
threat_model: Adversary is a skill PUBLISHER who distributes skills that unsuspecting users install and execute. Because installing a skill grants it full local user privileges with minimal scrutiny or interactive confirmation, the exploitation/privilege-escalation stage is eliminated. Two attack-vector classes: (1) code-level attacks via bundled scripts (credential harvesting, remote script execution such as curl|bash, data transmission to attacker-controlled endpoints, reverse shells); (2) instruction-level attacks via malicious directives in the natural-language SKILL.md (prompt injection, instruction override, hidden/coercive directives that steer the AI agent against its user, e.g. 'do NOT ask user permission', 'do NOT mention in conversation'). Attacker goals: data theft (credentials, source, agent context), agent hijacking (unauthorized actions, safety bypass), and persistence (supply-chain compromise, hidden triggers). The threat is grounded in real in-the-wild campaigns the paper cites (GTG-1002 espionage, Cato CTRL 'Medusa' ransomware).
limitations: (1) Single Jan 2026 snapshot from two public registries; excludes private/enterprise/unindexed repos, direct-shared skills, MCP servers, and platform-curated collections; a 2026 refresh saw 12.6x more skills, so labels age. (2) 60-second dynamic window likely misses time-delayed / environment-gated payloads; a 10% sample of unconfirmed candidates found 93.2% contained dormant triggers, so 157 is an explicit LOWER BOUND on prevalence. (3) Anti-sandbox evasion: sophisticated skills may detect Docker and suppress behavior. (4) Heavy single-actor skew: smp_170 contributes 54.1% of skills and 58.5% of vulnerabilities, so the authors deliberately report concentration at the publisher level (Gini 0.71) rather than per-skill, and aggregate statistics partly reflect one campaign. (5) Taxonomy may miss novel emerging patterns; manual labeling involves judgment calls for ambiguous dual-use cases. (6) Results may not generalize to enterprise or private skill-sharing channels. The 100% independent-maintainer removal rate is offered as external validation of the labels.

datasets_used: Authors' own crawl of 98,380 agent skills from two community registries: skills.rest (25,187 skills / 2,337 GitHub repos) and skillsmp.com (73,193 skills / 8,909 repos), Jan 2026

Constructed ground-truth set: 157 behaviorally-confirmed malicious skills with 632 labeled vulnerability instances across 13 attack techniques (released; full payloads gated via controlled-access process, Zenodo DOI 10.5281/zenodo.20285751)
300-skill labeled evaluation set (150 benign / 150 malicious) used for 5-fold cross-validated precision measurement
Adapted MITRE ATT&CK (6 kill-chain phases) and OWASP Top 10 for Agentic Applications used as the classification/severity frameworks
quantitative_results: Funnel: 98,380 skills -> 4,287 static candidates (4.4%) -> 762 runtime-triggered -> 157 confirmed malicious (0.16% of corpus, 3.7% of candidates)
632 labeled vulnerabilities; avg 4.03 per skill, median 4; 13 of 14 patterns appeared (E4 network reconnaissance absent from all confirmed cases)
Severity: CRITICAL 252 (39.9%), HIGH 202 (32.0%), MEDIUM 176 (27.8%), LOW 2 (0.3%); 71.8% CRIT+HIGH
Top techniques: SC2 Remote Script Execution 25.2% (159), P4 Behavior Manipulation 18.8% (119), E2 Credential Harvesting 17.7% (112), E1 External Transmission 13.6% (86)
84.2% of vulnerabilities (532/632) reside in SKILL.md natural-language files; executable code 8.5%; config/other 7.3%
Pipeline precision 99.6% (5-fold CV, 0.6 false positives per fold, <1 per 60 skills); claimed 90x over strongest static-only baseline (<=1.1% precision, Appendix F.2)
Kill-chain coverage: median 3 of 6 phases (mean 2.9); Execution 75.2%, Impact 69.4%, Credential Access 68.2%, Exfiltration 57.3%, Defense Evasion 15.9%, Reconnaissance 7.6%

Sophistication: Level 1 Basic 25 (15.9%), Level 2 Intermediate 122 (77.7%), Level 3 Advanced 10 (6.4%); vuln density 2.80->4.01->7.30, phases 1.23->3.10->5.10, E2->E1 chain 3.8%->39.3%->90.0%, shadow features 0%->86.1%->100%
Archetypes: Data Thieves 110 skills (70.1%, SC2 without P1), Agent Hijackers 16 skills (10.2%, P1 without SC2), 8 hybrids (5.1%); SC2<->P1 OR=0.11, p<0.001, phi=-0.41
E2->E1 data-exfil chain co-occurs in 58/157 skills (36.9%), OR=2.24, Fisher p=0.020

smp_170 factory: 85 brand-impersonation skills = 54.1% of malicious skills and 58.5% of vulnerabilities (370 instances); E2+SC2 fingerprint OR=556.1 (97.6% sensitivity, 83/85 vs 5/72); 100% template consistency, 26 identical lines, 85 brands across 15 sectors; per-publisher Gini 0.71

Shadow features in 115/157 skills (73.2%): undocumented endpoints 47.2%, conditional triggers 18.4%, obfuscated segments 11.0%, invisible-markup instructions 6.7%, other 16.7%; code-level obfuscation SC3 only 9.5% (15 skills)
Inter-rater: Cohen kappa 0.89 (malicious confirmation), 0.91 (vulnerability labeling), 0.83 (shadow features), 0.87 (recall sampling); blinded Fleiss kappa 0.85
Responsible disclosure: 147/157 removed at submission, all 157/157 (100%) removed/de-indexed after continued maintainer review
baselines_compared: Static-only detection baseline: <=1.1% precision (Appendix F.2) vs the paper's 99.6% -> claimed ~90x precision improvement

Cited prior static approaches (context, not head-to-head on this dataset): Liu et al. flagged 26.1% of skills as potentially risky; Cisco Skill Scanner flagged 10.7% as CRITICAL -- both criticized for not confirming intent/execution
Cross-ecosystem qualitative comparisons: browser extensions circa 2012, sophisticated Android malware lifecycle coverage, npm/PyPI supply-chain attacks (dependency confusion, typosquatting)
Cited GPT-Store study (Shen et al., 755,297 GPTs, 2,051 violations) noted as server-side and lacking the local-privilege surface -- related work, not a baseline the paper reproduces
